AI Case Study

Jira Summarizer with Microsoft Copilot RAG

A Technical Case Study — Agent Alpha

by Vyshnavi Gurram – Technical Writer

Introduction

This case study details the creation of Agent Alpha, an internal AI assistant built using Microsoft Copilot Studio. I used Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to design the agent as a bridge between two critical but separate data systems — static product documentation in SharePoint (User Guides, Admin Guides, and FAQs) and live project data in Jira.

What is RAG?

In RAG, the LLM does not depend solely on what it was trained on to answer questions. Instead, it retrieves relevant content from a connected knowledge source first, then uses that content to generate a response. This is what makes the agent's answers specific to company documentation rather than general.

The goal was simple: provide employees with a single point of reference to answer product questions and summarise technical tickets instantly.

Problem

Before Agent Alpha, Product Owners, Developers, and QA professionals faced a constant context-switching problem. To understand a feature, analyse related tickets, enhance a feature, or duplicate features across modules, they had to search both SharePoint and Jira manually. This process was slow and created unnecessary friction in daily workflows.

Solution

I built Agent Alpha to handle two primary functions — without requiring manual coding for query routing:

Knowledge Retrieval

The agent identifies product queries and pulls answers directly from the SharePoint knowledge base.

Jira Summarization

When provided with a Jira key, the agent retrieves, structures, and summarises ticket details — including descriptions, status, modules, and comments.

Implementation

The agent was built in two phases:

Phase 1 — Orchestration and Knowledge Base

I created the agent in Copilot Studio and enabled Generative AI Orchestration. This means the agent decides which tool to call based on what the user asks, rather than following a fixed flow. I then linked the SharePoint folders to the Knowledge Base so the agent could scan manuals and return relevant information.

Phase 2 — API Integration

I configured the "Get issue by key (V2)" tool to connect the agent to the organisation's Jira Cloud instance. The key part of this step was refining the tool's trigger logic — I configured the agent to recognise specific patterns such as ticketId-7572. This ensures the agent triggers an API call instead of a general document search.

Conversation Flow & Agent Testing

The agent includes built-in standard topics for Greetings and Small Talk to keep conversations natural and professional. I validated the agent logic through three distinct scenarios:

Scenario 1 — Small Talk
AgentHi, I am Agent Alpha, a virtual assistant. Just so you are aware, I sometimes use AI to answer questions.
(Result of enabling Generative Orchestration)
MeHi
AgentHello, how can I help you today?
Scenario 2 — RAG Search
MeTell me about teller receipts
AgentShows information retrieved from the Teller Poster User Guide by scanning SharePoint correctly.
Scenario 3 — API Trigger
MeGive me details of HELPHG-8942
AgentSummarises ticket details using a predefined structure — successfully bypassing documentation to pull directly from the Jira API.

Challenges

Although Microsoft Copilot Studio provides a built-in RAG environment, there were technical difficulties during development.

I initially attempted a manual connection to the Jira API by creating agent flows using HTTPS links and OAuth 2.0. When these manual attempts failed to retrieve correct details within the Copilot environment, I transitioned to the built-in Jira V2 connector. This shift provided the necessary stability.

Licensing Note

The agent was not published due to pricing constraints. The licensing cost makes it harder to justify for smaller teams, but the value is clear at scale.

Results

The project successfully built an agent that can distinguish between a general knowledge request and an API call query. By applying RAG, the agent provides company-specific, accurate information — significantly reducing the time staff spent switching between SharePoint and Jira manually.
